home.taglineSub: Document, share and track the architectural choices that matter.
home.feature1Desc: Record the context, options considered and consequences of each technical decision.
home.feature2Desc: Group decisions by project and keep everything tidy and accessible.
home.feature3Desc: Collaborate with your team and keep everyone aligned on the choices made.

hero.eyebrow: Technical decision log for modern teams
hero.headline: Keep AI-generated code aligned with your technical decisions
hero.subheadline: You write down what you decided. Decern links it to every pull request and checks the code change against it. If the change goes beyond what was approved (Terraform, dependencies, scope creep), the gate blocks before merge.
hero.bullet1: One place for every decision. One gate in CI. No more drift between docs and code.
hero.bullet2: First we check: is there a decision, and is it approved? Then we check: does this code change fit that decision?
hero.bullet3: When the change doesn't fit, the gate blocks. You get a clear reason. Only what you approved gets through.

problem.title: Decisions on paper. Code that drifts.
problem.intro: Your team's decisions live in Confluence, Notion, a doc, or meeting notes. When a PR lands, nobody checks if the code still matches what you decided. That means rework, bugs, and risk. With more AI-generated code, it gets worse: the AI doesn't know your rules. So you lose control just when you need it most.
problem.example1: A PR adds a new layer and bypasses the pattern you agreed on. Who catches it?
problem.example2: You refactor to remove an old approach. The next PR brings it back. Nobody was checking.
problem.example3: AI writes a lot of code fast. Zero memory of your decisions. The cost of drift goes up.

solution.adrIntro: Decern uses Architecture Decision Records (ADRs): short notes that say what you decided and why. Each record follows a simple structure: context, decision, consequences. So everyone can read and update it. This format was proposed by Michael Nygard. Read the original article to learn more.
solution.title: Four simple steps
solution.step1Body: Generate your decision with AI from meeting notes or a short brief. You review and approve. The thinking stays human. Context, options, what you chose, why. One place for everything.
solution.step2Body: In the PR title or description, point to that decision (e.g. ADR-001). The PR carries the decision. Your pipeline knows which rules apply.
solution.step3Body: decern-gate runs in CI (npx decern-gate). First it checks that the decision exists and is approved. Then AI compares the diff to the decision text. If the change is out of scope or contradicts what you decided, the gate blocks.
solution.step4Title: Block when the change doesn't fit
solution.flowStep1Desc: Generate with AI from notes or a brief. You review and approve. One place for everything.
solution.flowStep2Desc: Point to the decision in the PR. The pipeline knows which rules apply.
solution.flowStep3Desc: Gate runs in CI. Checks decision exists and is approved, then compares diff to decision.
solution.flowStep4Desc: The LLM as a Judge compares the diff to the decision. Change out of scope? Gate blocks with a clear reason. Only what matches gets through.
solution.step4Body: The decision can be approved and the gate can still block. Example: "ADR-001 approves only the new API. The diff changes Terraform and package.json. Out of scope." So only changes that match the decision get through.

ai.title: We check the code against your decision
ai.intro: Decern doesn't just ask "is there a decision?". We take the decision text and the PR diff and ask: does this change fit what you decided? If the code goes beyond scope or conflicts with the decision, we block it.
ai.point1: The decision can be approved and linked. We still block when the change doesn't fit the decision.
ai.point2: We tell you why. Example: "ADR-001 approves only the new API. The diff changes Terraform and package.json. Out of scope."

judge.title: Your model. Your control.
judge.intro: The Judge that compares your diff to the decision runs on the LLM you configure—OpenAI, Claude, or any OpenAI-compatible API. You choose the provider and the model.
judge.point1: You choose the provider and model. OpenAI, Claude, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. The Judge uses your configuration for that request only.
judge.point2: We never store or log your API key. It is sent only for the Judge request and never retained.

features.title: Built for clarity and control
features.f1Desc: Context, options, what you chose, why. One source of truth. No more scattered docs or threads.
features.f2Desc: Point to a decision in the PR title or description. Your pipeline knows which rules apply to each change.
features.f3Title: LLM as a Judge in CI
features.f3Desc: The Judge compares the code change to the decision text. Out of scope or scope creep (e.g. "new API only" but the PR touches Terraform)? The gate blocks with a clear reason. No regex, just reasoning.
features.f4Desc: Organize decisions by topic. The right rules apply to each PR.
features.f5Desc: Workspaces, projects, roles. The right people own and approve decisions.
features.f6Desc: Over time you see where and when decisions were broken. Fix drift before it becomes a mess.

useCases.title: Use cases
useCases.uc1Title: Tech leads who want to scale without losing control
useCases.uc1Desc: You write down the rules. The gate enforces them. So you can move fast without becoming the bottleneck.
useCases.uc2Title: Teams that use AI to write code
useCases.uc2Desc: Generated code goes through the same checks as the rest. You trust it because it's checked against your decisions.
useCases.uc3Desc: New PRs can't bring back patterns you're removing. The gate keeps everyone on the same direction.
useCases.uc4Desc: Show what was decided and how it was enforced in CI. Clear evidence when you need it.

trust.title: For tech leads who care about control
trust.claim1: You already write down decisions. Decern connects them to your code and enforces them in CI. No extra paperwork.
trust.claim2: We're the only gate that checks the actual code change against the decision. Not just "is there a decision?" but "does this change fit it?"

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.q1: How is this different from Confluence, Notion, or ADRs in Markdown?
faq.a1: Those tools store documents. They don't enforce them. Decern links decisions to PRs and runs checks in CI. If a PR breaks a decision, the pipeline can block or flag it. You also get a structured log you can search and report on.
faq.q2: How is this different from code review or static analysis?
faq.a2: Review and linters don't know your architectural decisions. Decern does. We check each PR against what you decided. One extra question: "Does this respect our decision?"
faq.q3: Does the AI write the decisions for us?
faq.a3: You can use AI to draft a decision (e.g. from meeting notes). A human reviews and approves. Decern enforces what humans decided. It doesn't replace decision-making.
faq.q4: Won't this add bureaucracy or block us too often?
faq.a4: Almost no change to your workflow: one link in the PR, one step in CI. You choose: the gate can block (strict) or only report (observation). On Free we only report, so you can try it without blocking releases.
faq.q5: Do we need to change our GitHub or GitLab workflow?
faq.a5: No. Add a link to the decision in the PR and one step in CI (e.g. npx decern-gate). Your process stays the same.

cta.title: Connect your decisions to your code. Enforce them in CI.
cta.subline: One decision log. Linked to PRs. Gate that checks every change.
cta.ctaPrimary: Try Decern free with your team
